slides.title: The Big Three: OS Ecosystems
slides.content: Operating Systems (OS) are the fundamental software layers that manage computer hardware resources and provide common services for computer programs. In the modern engineering landscape, three major ecosystems dominate the field. Linux is the undisputed engine of the web, powering the vast majority of servers, Android devices, and Docker containers due to its open-source nature and extreme modularity. Windows remains the backbone of the enterprise world and high-end gaming, offering deep integration with legacy systems and the most comprehensive driver support. macOS, built on a solid Unix foundation (XNU kernel), provides a polished, high-performance environment that is preferred by creative professionals and developers who demand both a powerful command-line interface and a seamless graphical user experience. Understanding these ecosystems is not just about choosing a tool; it is about understanding the distinct philosophies of software management and hardware abstraction that define how we build and deploy applications in a polyglot world.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f8fafc'/><circle cx='120' cy='100' r='40' fill='#facc15' fill-opacity='0.2'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='40' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.2'/><circle cx='280' cy='100' r='40' fill='#6366f1' fill-opacity='0.2'/><path d='M100 130 L140 130' stroke='#854d0e' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M180 130 L220 130' stroke='#1e40af' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M260 130 L300 130' stroke='#3730a3' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#64748b'>The OS Trinity: Diversified Abstraction</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does the Unix philosophy utilized in Linux and macOS differ from the architectural approach taken by Windows?
Explain the role of the Kernel in managing the relationship between application code and physical hardware.
Why is Linux the preferred choice for containerization and server-side engineering in 2026?

slides.title: Mobile Giants: Android & iOS
slides.content: Smartphones have evolved to become the primary computing device for most of the global population, and their operating systems follow the same core logic as desktop systems but are heavily optimized for touch interaction, energy efficiency, and mobile connectivity. Android, maintained by Google, is built on a Linux kernel and offers an open ecosystem where manufacturers can customize the experience according to their needs. It primarily utilizes Java and Kotlin for application development, leveraging a sophisticated virtual machine (ART) to manage memory and performance. In contrast, Apple's iOS is a tightly controlled 'walled garden' built on the XNU kernel. It prioritizes a high-fidelity user experience and strict privacy controls, with Swift and Objective-C serving as the primary development languages. For the modern full-stack engineer, understanding these mobile platforms is critical, as a significant portion of web traffic and application usage originates from these highly optimized, mobile-first environments.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><rect x='100' y='50' width='80' height='120' rx='10' fill='#22c55e' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='220' y='50' width='80' height='120' rx='10' fill='#64748b' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='140' cy='150' r='5' fill='#16a34a'/><circle cx='260' cy='150' r='8' fill='#475569' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='190' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#166534'>Mobile Ecosystem Dominance</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Compare the memory management strategies of the Android Runtime (ART) with the Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) used in iOS/Swift.
How does the 'Open' nature of Android impact security compared to the 'Closed' nature of iOS?
Discuss the challenges of building cross-platform applications that must run seamlessly on both Android and iOS kernels.

slides.title: The Hardware Trinity: CPU, RAM, Disk
slides.content: The execution of any piece of software, from a simple script to a complex AI agent, is dependent on the harmonious interaction of three fundamental hardware components: the CPU, RAM, and Persistent Storage. The Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the 'Brain' of the system, responsible for fetching, decoding, and executing instructions at billions of cycles per second. Random Access Memory (RAM) acts as the 'Volatile Workspace' or 'Desk Space,' providing high-speed access to data and instructions that are currently being used by active processes. Because RAM is volatile, all data is lost when power is removed. This necessitates the 'Warehouse'—Persistent Storage (HDD or SSD)—where the operating system, codebase, and user data are stored permanently. As a software architect, your job is to optimize the flow of data between these three components: minimizing CPU cycles through efficient algorithms, managing RAM usage to prevent swapping, and optimizing Disk I/O to ensure that your application remains responsive during heavy data loads.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fefce8'/><rect x='60' y='60' width='60' height='60' rx='4' fill='#eab308' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='160' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='4' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='280' y='60' width='60' height='80' rx='4' fill='#ef4444' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 90 H160 M240 90 H280' stroke='#64748b' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#854d0e'>The Cycle of Execution</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the 'Von Neumann Bottleneck' and how it impacts the relationship between the CPU and RAM.
Compare the 'Latency' of accessing data in L1 Cache, RAM, and an NVMe SSD.
How can a software developer intentionally reduce the 'Memory Footprint' of a long-running background process?

slides.title: Persistence & The Hard Disk
slides.content: Persistence is a core architectural requirement for any system that needs to maintain state over time. Unlike RAM, which is fast but fleeting, hard disks and solid-state drives are non-volatile storage mediums. Even when the system is powered down, the electrical or magnetic representation of your data remains intact. In the realm of modern software engineering, we have largely transitioned from rotating magnetic platters (HDDs) to Solid State Drives (SSDs) powered by NAND flash memory. This transition has dramatically reduced 'Latency' and increased 'Throughput,' allowing for almost instantaneous loading of operating systems and massive databases. For a developer, optimizing for 'Disk I/O' involves understanding how data is buffered and written to the physical medium—utilizing techniques like synchronous vs. asynchronous writes to ensure that the application doesn't freeze while waiting for the 'Warehouse' to confirm a storage operation. In our stack, the `database.ts` file acts as the primary orchestrator that bridges our volatile logic with this persistent layer.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f5f3ff'/><path d='M120 60 H280 V140 H120 Z' fill='#8b5cf6' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#8b5cf6' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='30' fill='none' stroke='#8b5cf6' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M200 70 V130 M170 100 H230' stroke='#8b5cf6' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#5b21b6'>Non-Volatile Data Persistence</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is random I/O significantly faster on an SSD compared to an HDD?
Explain the concept of 'Write amplification' in modern flash-based storage devices.
How does the choice of a Filesystem (like ext4 vs ZFS) impact data integrity and performance on persistent storage?

slides.title: The Command Line Interface (CLI)
slides.content: The Command Line Interface (CLI) is the traditional, text-based entrance to the operating system's internal power. While Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) are excellent for accessibility and creative work, the CLI is the native language of the professional engineer. It provides a direct, low-latency method of communicating with the OS kernel, enabling the automation of complex tasks that would be impossible to perform manually. By typing commands into a 'Shell' (like Bash or Zsh), you can manipulate the filesystem, manage running processes, and configure networking with surgical precision. Fundamental commands like `ls` (list files), `cd` (change directory), and `mkdir` (make directory) are the building blocks of this environment. In our FRAISE architecture, the CLI is not just an option; it is the primary way we interact with our Dockerized environments, allowing us to bootstrap entire service clusters with a single command line sequence.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='20' y='40' font-family='monospace' font-size='14' fill='#10b981'>$ ls -la /repo-php</text><text x='20' y='70' font-family='monospace' font-size='14' fill='#94a3b8'>drwxr-xr-x  admin  staff  (core)</text><text x='20' y='100' font-family='monospace' font-size='14' fill='#10b981'>$ ./entrypoint.sh</text><text x='20' y='130' font-family='monospace' font-size='12' fill='#fb7185'>[SYSTEM] Initializing Nginx proxy...</text><rect x='20' y='150' width='10' height='2' fill='#10b981'><animate attributeName='opacity' values='0;1;0' dur='1s' repeatCount='indefinite'/></rect></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is it considered 'best practice' to use the CLI for server administration instead of a remote desktop solution?
Compare the 'Shell' (interpreter) with the 'Terminal' (emulator). How do they interact?
How can you use command history and aliases to significantly increase your development velocity in the terminal?

slides.title: Pipes & Redirection: The Unix Philosophy
slides.content: The Unix philosophy is built on the idea of 'making each program do one thing well' and then connecting those programs together. The most powerful manifestation of this is the Pipe (`|`). A pipe takes the standard output (`stdout`) of one command and feeds it directly as the standard input (`stdin`) of another. This allows you to build complex data processing pipelines from simple, atomic tools. For example, `ps aux | grep node` allows you to list every running process on the system and then instantly filter that list for only those containing the word 'node.' Combined with redirection operators like `>` (save to file) and `>>` (append to file), you can capture, transform, and persist data across the entire system with minimal effort. Mastering pipes and redirection is the first step toward becoming a 'Terminal Power User' who can diagnose system issues and automate complex infrastructure workflows in seconds.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#0f172a'/><rect x='50' y='80' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='270' y='80' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#10b981' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M130 100 H270' stroke='#f472b6' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='10'><animate attributeName='stroke-dashoffset' from='20' to='0' dur='0.5s' repeatCount='indefinite'/></path><text x='90' y='105' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#38bdf8'>cat</text><text x='310' y='105' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#10b981'>grep</text><text x='200' y='150' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8'>The IO Stream Pipe</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the difference between `stdout` (File Descriptor 1) and `stderr` (File Descriptor 2) in the context of redirection.
How would you use a combination of `ls`, `grep`, and `wc` to count the number of `.php` files in a repository?
What is the 'Named Pipe' (FIFO) and how does it differ from the standard anonymous pipe used in shells?

slides.title: Processes vs. Threads
slides.content: Understanding the difference between a Process and a Thread is vital for building performant, multi-tasking applications. A Process is a completely independent instance of a program in execution, with its own isolated memory space. If one process crashes, it generally does not affect others—this is why our PHP backend and Node.js server run as separate processes in their own Docker containers. A Thread, on the other hand, is a 'sub-task' that lives within a process and shares that process's memory and resources. This sharing allows threads to communicate very quickly, but it also introduces the risk of 'Race Conditions' if multiple threads try to modify the same data at the same time. In our FRAISE stack, we leverage concurrency through both multi-processing (using Docker to isolate microservices) and multi-threading (in Go and Rust) to ensure our system can handle high traffic while maintaining perfect data integrity.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><rect x='60' y='60' width='120' height='100' rx='8' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='95' r='10' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.5'/><circle cx='120' cy='125' r='10' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.5'/><rect x='220' y='60' width='120' height='100' rx='8' fill='#10b981' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='280' cy='110' r='15' fill='#10b981' fill-opacity='0.5'/><text x='120' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#1e40af'>Process (Multi-Threaded)</text><text x='280' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#065f46'>Isolated Process</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why does Node.js use a single-threaded event loop instead of a multi-threaded approach for handling concurrent requests?
Explain the 'Context Switching' overhead when the OS moves the CPU between different processes.
How can shared memory between threads lead to data corruption? How do we use 'Mutexes' to prevent this?

slides.title: Memory Management & Virtual RAM
slides.content: Memory Management is the complex task performed by the operating system to ensure that every running program has enough space to work without interfering with others. The OS uses 'Memory Isolation' to prevent one process from reading or writing to the memory of another, which is a critical security feature. However, physical RAM is finite. To overcome this limitation, the OS utilizes **Virtual Memory**, a technique that uses a portion of the persistent storage (HDD/SSD) to simulate additional RAM. Through a process called 'Paging' or 'Swapping,' the OS moves inactive data from RAM to this virtual space and back again when needed. While this prevents the system from crashing when RAM is full, it significantly slows down performance because the disk is much slower than RAM. As an engineer, monitoring your application's 'Memory Footprint' is essential to avoid the dreaded 'Thrashing'—a state where the system spends all its time moving data between RAM and Disk instead of executing your code.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fff7ed'/><rect x='80' y='40' width='100' height='120' rx='4' fill='#f97316' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#f97316' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='220' y='80' width='100' height='80' rx='4' fill='#64748b' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#64748b' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M180 100 L220 100' stroke='#f97316' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'><animate attributeName='stroke-dashoffset' from='10' to='0' dur='1s' repeatCount='indefinite'/></path><text x='130' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#9a3412'>Physical RAM</text><text x='270' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#475569'>Virtual Swap Space</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does a 'Memory Leak' in a long-running process eventually lead to system instability?
Explain the role of the MMU (Memory Management Unit) hardware in the CPU.
What is the 'OOM Killer' in Linux and why is it necessary for server stability?

slides.title: I/O Redirection: Save Your Output
slides.content: I/O Redirection is the standard way to bridge the gap between volatile terminal output and persistent filesystem storage. In a Unix-like environment, every command has three default data streams: Standard Input (`stdin`), Standard Output (`stdout`), and Standard Error (`stderr`). By using redirection operators, you can send these streams to files instead of the terminal. The `>` operator creates a new file (or overwrites an existing one) with the command's output, while `>>` appends the output to the end of a file. For example, `echo 'Server Started' > server.log` initializes a log file, and `date >> server.log` adds a timestamp to it. This capability is fundamental for recording application logs, saving the output of database queries, and capturing system state for later analysis. In our `file_list.txt` example, we use redirection to record exactly which files were modified during a development turn, ensuring a perfect audit trail of our architectural changes.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#0f172a'/><path d='M100 80 L200 80' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M210 80 L230 80' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='3'/><rect x='240' y='60' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#10b981' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2'/><text x='140' y='105' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='14' fill='#10b981'>></text><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8'>Redirecting the bitstream</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How would you redirect both `stdout` and `stderr` to the same file using a single command?
Explain the danger of accidentally using `>` when you intended to use `>>` on a critical configuration file.
What is `/dev/null` and why do engineers frequently redirect output to this 'Black Hole' device?

slides.title: Filesystem & File Descriptors
slides.content: One of the most profound concepts in Unix and Linux is that **'Everything is a file'**. This means that not only are your `index.php` and `README.md` files, but so are your keyboard, your screen, and even your network connection. To the OS kernel, these are all just streams of bytes that can be read from or written to. A **File Descriptor (FD)** is the low-level integer ID that the kernel uses to keep track of these open streams. By default, every process starts with three fundamental FDs: `0` for `stdin` (keyboard), `1` for `stdout` (screen), and `2` for `stderr` (error logs). When your application opens a database file or a network socket, the OS assigns a new integer (like `3` or `4`). Understanding this abstraction allows you to treat complex I/O operations (like streaming media from an AI agent) with the same simple logic you use to read a text file, providing a unified and powerful mental model for all computing interactions.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='100' y='40' width='200' height='120' rx='8' fill='#64748b' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#64748b' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='70' font-family='monospace' font-size='12' fill='#334155'>0: stdin  -> Keyboard</text><text x='110' y='100' font-family='monospace' font-size='12' fill='#334155'>1: stdout -> Screen</text><text x='110' y='130' font-family='monospace' font-size='12' fill='#334155'>2: stderr -> Log File</text><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#64748b'>Unified File Descriptor Model</text></svg>
studentPrompts: If 'Everything is a file,' how does the OS distinguish between a regular file on disk and a physical hardware device?
What happens when a process reaches its 'Maximum File Descriptors' limit?
Explain how the `lsof` (List Open Files) command can be used to debug a networking issue.

slides.title: Networking: The OSI 7-Layer Model
slides.content: Networking is the complex science of moving data across a vast, interconnected landscape. To manage this complexity, engineers use the OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) 7-Layer model as a standardized map. Data travels from your application code down through the layers, across the physical wires, and back up the layers on the receiving machine. The most critical layers for software engineers are Layer 3 (Network), where routing and IP addresses live; Layer 4 (Transport), which handles reliable delivery via protocols like TCP; and Layer 7 (Application), where the logic of HTTP, SSH, and DNS resides. In our FRAISE stack, we largely work at Layer 7 by writing Node.js and PHP logic, but we must understand Layer 4 to configure our Docker port mappings and Layer 3 to manage the communication between our microservices. This layered approach ensures that a change in the physical wire (Layer 1) doesn't require a total rewrite of our application code.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><rect x='100' y='20' width='200' height='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.2'/><rect x='100' y='45' width='200' height='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.15'/><rect x='100' y='70' width='200' height='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.1'/><rect x='100' y='95' width='200' height='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.08'/><rect x='100' y='120' width='200' height='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.05'/><text x='200' y='35' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#1e40af'>L7: Application (HTTP)</text><text x='200' y='110' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#1e40af'>L4: Transport (TCP)</text><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#1e40af'>The Networking Stack Hierarchy</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is HTTP considered a Layer 7 protocol if it relies on TCP at Layer 4?
Explain the concept of 'Encapsulation' as data moves down the OSI layers.
What is the difference between a 'Hub' (Layer 1), a 'Switch' (Layer 2), and a 'Router' (Layer 3)?

slides.title: TCP vs. UDP: Reliability vs. Speed
slides.content: At Layer 4 of the networking stack, two primary protocols define how data is transported: TCP and UDP. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is built for absolute reliability. It uses a 'Three-Way Handshake' to establish a connection and meticulously checks that every packet arrives in the correct order. If a packet is lost, TCP automatically requests a retransmission. This makes it ideal for web traffic (HTTP), emails, and secure access (SSH), where a single missing bit could corrupt the entire communication. In contrast, UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is built for raw speed. It sends data 'packets' into the network and immediately forgets about them, with no confirmation of delivery. This 'Fire and Forget' approach is perfect for real-time streaming, gaming, and VoIP calls where a split-second delay is worse than a slightly fuzzy image. Understanding this trade-off is critical when designing the real-time features of the FRAISE platform, ensuring we choose the right protocol for the specific task at hand.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf2f8'/><path d='M100 80 Q150 40 200 80 T300 80' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><path d='M100 120 L300 120' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='10'/><text x='100' y='70' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#be185d'>TCP: Reliable Handshake</text><text x='100' y='145' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#be185d'>UDP: High-Velocity Stream</text><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#831843'>Transport Protocol Trade-offs</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why would you almost never use UDP for a database synchronization task?
Explain the 'SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK' handshake sequence in TCP.
How do modern protocols like QUIC attempt to combine the reliability of TCP with the speed of UDP?

slides.title: Ports: The Logical Doors
slides.content: If you think of an IP address as a building's street address, a **Port** is the specific apartment or office number within that building. Every computer can have 65,535 logical ports, allowing it to run many different network services at the same time on a single IP. Some ports are standardized by the IANA: Port 80 is for unencrypted web traffic (HTTP), Port 443 is for secure web traffic (HTTPS), and Port 22 is for remote terminal access (SSH). Development servers frequently use non-standard ports like 3000 or 8080 to avoid conflicts with system services. In our `docker-compose.yml`, we use port mapping to connect the external traffic of the host machine to the internal ports of our isolated containers. Understanding ports is fundamental for securing your application, as a 'Port Scan' is the first tool an attacker uses to find open, potentially vulnerable doors into your infrastructure.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><rect x='80' y='60' width='40' height='60' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='60' width='40' height='60' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='200' y='60' width='40' height='60' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='280' y='60' width='60' height='60' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='140' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#115e59'>80</text><text x='160' y='140' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#115e59'>443</text><text x='220' y='140' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#115e59'>22</text><text x='310' y='140' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace' font-size='10' fill='#115e59'>3000</text><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#115e59'>Logical Network Entrypoints</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is the difference between 'Well-Known Ports' (0-1023) and 'Ephemeral Ports'?
Explain how the `netstat` command can be used to see which ports are currently 'Listening' on your machine.
How does a Firewall use port numbers to prevent unauthorized access to a server?

slides.title: SSH: Secure Shell
slides.content: SSH (Secure Shell) is the industry-standard cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network. It is the invisible umbilical cord that connects an engineer to their remote servers, allowing them to open a terminal, execute commands, and transfer files as if they were sitting directly in front of the machine. SSH uses 'Asymmetric Encryption' (Public/Private key pairs) to ensure that even if an attacker intercepts the traffic, they cannot read the data or steal the login credentials. Beyond simple shell access, SSH provides powerful features like 'Port Forwarding,' which allows you to tunnel local traffic through a secure connection to a remote database. In professional DevOps workflows, manual passwords are rarely used; instead, we use `authorized_keys` to provide instant, key-based access to entire clusters of machines halfway across the world with mathematical certainty of identity.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#0f172a'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='60' fill='none' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='10'><animateTransform attributeName='transform' type='rotate' from='0 200 100' to='360 200 100' dur='10s' repeatCount='indefinite'/></circle><path d='M180 100 L200 80 L220 100 L200 120 Z' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8'>Encrypted Remote Orchestration</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the benefit of using 'SSH Keys' over standard 'Username/Password' authentication.
What is 'SSH Agent Forwarding' and why is it useful when jumping between multiple remote servers?
How can you use `ssh-keygen` to generate a secure Ed25519 key pair for your development machine?

slides.title: DNS: The Phonebook of the Internet
slides.content: The Domain Name System (DNS) is the critical infrastructure that translates human-readable names like `google.com` or `site1.com` into the numerical IP addresses (like `142.250.190.46`) that computers use to find each other on the internet. Without DNS, we would have to memorize long strings of numbers for every website we visit. DNS is a vast, hierarchical, and decentralized database system where 'Authoritative Nameservers' hold the official records for specific domains. When you type a URL into your browser, a 'Recursive Resolver' (usually provided by your ISP) journeys across the internet to find the correct IP address in a matter of milliseconds. In our FRAISE environment, we use Nginx to map these logical names to our internal container IPs, mimicking the behavior of a global DNS system within our local development cluster. Understanding DNS records (like A, CNAME, and MX) is vital for any developer who needs to launch and manage live web applications in the real world.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf4ff'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='50' fill='#a21caf' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M160 100 H240 M200 60 V140' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#701a75' font-weight='600'>google.com -> 142.250.190.46</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is the difference between an 'A Record' and a 'CNAME Record' in a DNS configuration?
Explain the role of the 'TTL' (Time to Live) value in DNS caching and domain propagation.
How does 'DNS Hijacking' work and what are the security measures (like DNSSEC) used to prevent it?
